title_tesim: Jean Moorhead posing on a "surfboard," Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Jean Moorhead model. 20th Century Fox, Elks 906, 1951. Composite Elks convention.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Jean Moorhead, wearing a stripped bathing suit, posing as if riding a surfboard. Moorhead is actually standing on a ironing board situated on a lawn with foliage, flowering shrubs, and a chain link fence seen in the background. A portion of an open newspaper is visible in the foreground.
Barbara Jean Moorhead was an actress and model. She was Playboy Playmate of the Month, October 1955 (as Jean Moorehead).

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada
View from Spanish-tile roof with decorative dovecote, with tops of palm trees in foreground, valley, mountains, and cloudy sky in background
combined_subject_ssim: Hotel Playa de Ensenada (Ensenada, Baja California, Mexico)

title_tesim: Pico House and Merced Theater, El Pueblo de Los Angeles Historical Monument, Los Angeles, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2022 - 2023 Old Pico House, Los Angeles.  213R1694, 4, TLW.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Still standing, opposite the Plaza is the old Pico House, now the National Hotel, which served as the city's most elaborate hostelery a half century ago.  It was the terminus of the Los Angeles-San Francisco six horse stages before the railways.  To the extreme right of the hotel is the first theater, the Merced, opened in 1870 in Los Angeles.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photograph of Doble Mine, San Bernardino County, 1930
caption_tesim: Text readst: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th St, Santa Monica, Cal.  High above Baldwin Lake in the San Bernardino Mountains of Southern California stands the bleaching mine buildings and cyanide tanks of the famous Doble Mine which for thirty years prior to the World War was worked by the famous Western sportsman and millionaire, the late E.J. (Lucky) Baldwin, and has produced, it is stated, upwards of 20 million dollars in gold.  But due to litigation among heirs to Baldwin's millions the famous mine has been closed for many years, although the mountains in the immediate vicinity are said to be rich in the yellow material. E. J. (Lucky) Baldwin, who formerly operated themine, was one of the best known figures in the West. He  literally made money at everything he undertook, whether it was race-tracks, farming, mining or whatnot. His mountain home at Talac on Lake Tahoe in North Central California (see recent description by Will Rogers in Hearst Sunday Magazine) is one of the show places of the west. But the Baldwin heirs can very well get along without any additional gold which may be forthcoming from the Doble Mine, for dufing the real estate and oil booms in Southern California the family, according to reputable bankers, has amassed an additional 100 million in assets. The name of Baldwin M. Baldwin, a grandson of E.J. (Lucky) Baldwin, was linked with that of Suzanne Lenglen during her last tennis tour of the U.S.
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1980 Baldwin Lake

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2593. Children's Home L.A.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Photograph of a children's table and chairs. The table is set with a place mat (embroidered with ducks), a plate, cup, and utensils. The chair in front of the place setting is empty, while the chair to its left is occupied by a doll.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

title_tesim: Typewritten document describing photograph of American women on trial in Bursa, Turkey, 1928
summary_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2412 Lucille Day, Pac Palisades, 1927.  Brausa.  Turkish court with Miss Jennie Jilson writing, Lucille Day & Edith Sanderson in front - next to last day of trial.  Dist Court of Brausa, Turkey Apr 25/28 Judge Ahmed Nizameddin - sentenced 3 girls 3 days in jail fine 13.00.  Miss Day now at Salonica
caption_tesim: Text reads: Actual photograph, said to be the first released in this country, showing the Turkish court at Brousa, Turkey, April 25, 1928, before Judge Ahmed Nisameddin, when the Misses Lucille Day of Pacific Palisades, Cal., Edith Sanderson, Berkeley, Cal., and M. Jennie Jilson, American girl missionary workers, each were sentenced to serve 3 days in jail and to pay a fine of $13 for alleged teaching of Christianity, contrary to present Turkish religious laws. Left to right, the missionaries are, Miss Day (wearing glasses), Miss Sanderson and Miss Jilson (writing). Judge Nisameddin sentenced the missionaries. Then they appealed to a higher court. After a long period of waiting the case came up before the court of appeals. It decided to retry the case before Judge Nisameddin who immediately reaffirmed the original punishment. Sentence was passed at Constantinople, Sept. 26, 1928, according to an associated dispatch received recently, for teaching religion. Mrs. W. H. Day, Pacific Palisades, Calif., mother of Lucille Day, upon receipt of information covering the reaffirmed sentence said: “The girls really were arrested for pausing in silence with heads bent, before sitting down at the dinner table and for wearing their best dresses on Sunday, when Saturday is the Mohammedan day of rest.” Miss Day is now in Salonica, Greece, teaching at the American school. The Miss Sanderson and Jilson are in Italy and plan to return to the States.

title_tesim: May Betteridge and avocados, Los Angeles, 1933
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2661 P1 to 7.  Calavo - Avacado [sic] Industry Growers of California.  11/1/33.  May Betteridge
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002bddtt (document)
description_tesim: Possibly montage photo of May Betteridge, representing the California avocado industry, with tray and basket of avocados, shelf of avocados in foreground, crates of avocados labeled Calavo [for Calavo Growers of California] in background. The avocado varieties shown are (left to right): Pueblo, Topa Topa, Dickinson, Itzanana, Fuerte and Linscott
May Betteridge appears in Los Angeles Times reportage from 1929 to 1934 and again in 1949-1950. She was known for her "little-theater" work by 1928 and was the National Orange Show Queen in 1934.

description_tesim: Text with notes on photographs taken  documenting the San Diego Mission and related mission-era monuments and artifacts

Text reads: Inscription at base of cross / at Fray Junipero Museum, Presidio Hill, / Old Town, San Diego, Calif.: In this ancient Indian village of Cosoy, discovered and named San Miguel by Cabrillo in 1542, visited by and christened San Diego De Alcala by Vizcaino in 1602. Here the first citizen Fray Junipero Serra planted civilization in California. Here he raised the cross. Here began the first Mission. Here founded the first town – San Diego, in July 16, 1769. Notes: San Diego Mission 1769. The Bible that Father Serra taught from and wrote? Olive trees and palm trees the Padre Serra planted. Old Fort - Presidion Hill - Presidio Park - California Museum. Ramona's Marriage Place. also Casa de Machado, built 1801, oldest adobe in California.

caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Pasadena Rose Tourney, year 1927; 2081 2082, 2083, 2084, 2085, 2086, 2087, 2088, 2089, 2090, 2091, 2092

description_tesim: Czechoslovakia was the first foreign government to enter a float in the Parade, in 1927.
1927 was the first year the the Tournament of Roses Parade had a theme, "Songs in Flowers," with each float typifying a song

A Tournament of the Roses parade float with a European theme (probably the entry from Czechoslovakia). The participant furthest to the left is wearing a traditional Eastern-European dress, while the three participants to the right of the float are wearing gowns. The float is decorated with palms and other various foliage, and a two-dimensional sun is suspended within an arch flanked by monumental vases of flowers. The Pasadena Memorial Flagpole (Goodhue Flagpole) is visible behind the float in its original location in the middle of the intersection of Orange Grove and Colorado Boulevard.

title_tesim: Typewritten letter to Adelbert Bartlett from Mertens and Price, Inc. requesting Sunday Players photographs, 1938
caption_tesim: Mr. Adelbert Bartlett, 2432 Third Street, Santa Monica, California.Dear Mr. Bartlett,May we have a dozen prints of the composite used on the Christmas booklet, enclosed? We’d have liked to develop something new this year, but even with your warning card Christmas sort of “snuck up” on us… even now it doesn’t seem possible and this photo will do very well. I still think it’s a masterpiece. Also six each of the following (not quite so rush): Composite which we made for Easter, of Charles Anderson as Roman Soldier, with lightening. The Three Marys…2594 P. 12, P. 10; Samson & Delilah…P 23; Group …P 9;Pharaoh…P  30; Mnerris Pleads with Pharaoh… P  19; Sadie Crockett as Leah…  P  36; Louis Merrill as Samson…P  31;The Two Apostles…P  5; Victoria Vola as Rachel …P  32; Potiphar’s Wife Accuses Joseph…P  28; Vola as Rebecca…P  34;Judas…P  4. Yours very truly, MERTENS AND PRICE. BMF. cc 1572 West Pico.

title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement with daisies by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2595 P 22 to 35 Preininger, Flowers, Los Angeles Junior College, Selected, 1935
Handwritten at edge of negative: 2595 50
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Daisies and leaves in shallow bowl with crackled glaze on small table

Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

title_tesim: Eugene R. Plummer and 2 women, West Hollywood, 1927
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2361 - 2362 - 2 [sleeve torn, text missing] Eugene R. Plu [sleeve torn, text missing] Hollywood, Thomas Workman Temple. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. Col. Eugene R. Plummer, Hollywood's oldest settler, showing him with 1- On horseback with women members of family, descendents [sic] of Spanish pioneers 2- With guitar and Indian relics 3- Ranch windmill and patio of home. El Rey de la Vaqueros, Aug 1927
named_subject_tesim: Eugene R. Plummer residence (West Hollywood, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/ 21198/zz002bdmn4 (document)
Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/ 21198/zz002bdmrp (document)
ark:/ 21198/zz002bdms6 (document)
description_tesim: Eugene R. Plummer and his family once owned sections of Los Angeles including the Hancock Park area, mid-Wilshire, Hollywood Hills, and West Hollywood. The site of his home was purchased by the County of Los Angeles in 1937 and is known as Plummer Park, West Hollywood.
Eugene R. Plummer, seated in garden of his home, with 2 unidentified women, 1 seated, 1 standing with watering can, among trees, potted plants, and fence
combined_subject_ssim: Eugene R. Plummer residence (West Hollywood, Calif.)

description_tesim: View towards a Victorian house on an embankment elevated above the street level. The house has a square tower with a sign reading "Apartments" and a wrap around porch. In 1887 Banning purchased a beer garden at from Jacob Philippi and converted the complex into her home

title_tesim: Hand-colored sample print with view towards the Boğaziçi Üniversitesi campus (formerly part of Robert College), Istanbul, 1925 or 1935
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve:  Robert College, Constantinople, 1925, 8 x 10 neg. of panorama, 8 x 10 good frmt to copy, 2092 1/2, A. Bartlett in Turkey, year 1935
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002bd3mc (document)
description_tesim: Robert College is a school located on a sixty-five acre wooded campus on the European side of Istanbul between the two bridges on the Bosphorus. It was founded in 1863 by Americans, Christopher Rhinelander Robert and Cyrus Hamlin. In 1972 the higher education division was transferred to the Turkish government and has continued as Boğaziçi University, Turkey's leading public university.

View towards the Boğaziçi Üniversitesi campus (formerly part of Robert College), and the towers of the Rumeli Hisari (fortress) beyond, facing south across the Bosphorus. This is a sample print, colored by Bartlett to instruct the colorist.

title_tesim: Text describing a hand-colored sample photographic of a view towards the Boğaziçi Üniversitesi campus (formerly part of Robert College), Istanbul, 1925 or 1935
caption_tesim: Text: Subject is: Robert (American) College, in the Bosphorus, Constantinople looking south into Asia. Wanted: A general effect not too much detailed coloring,</emph> approximately as suggested on 10 inch proof print. Colors: Trees that frame picture; dark green. Foreground: center, field of ripe grain, not too prominent, darker than proof, perhaps.  suggest shocks of sheaves standing in field, to relieve blankness contrasted with field of green grain on hill above. Trees on campus: are dark green masses. Buildings: For most part grayish-bluish-green, except at left which are perhaps cream stucco. Tennis courts in center show golden in sunlight. Towers by water’s edge are old brownish yellow (erected year 1452). WATER IS DEEP BOSPHORUS, almost like sample, darker perhaps on offshore. Buildings opposite shore pink and yellow. Forests back of shore include some dark green cypresses, suggest these. Fields on opp. Shore show ripening grain to burnt brown. Hillsides are burnt brown to first horizon. Distant horizon shades into violet – perhaps. Sky is medium blue, but not dark enough to draw interest from DEEP BLUE water. Use plenty of color.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002bd3kv (negative)
description_tesim: Typewritten and annotated instructions for a photograph colorist, indicating what colors to use in different areas of the photograph. Bartlett also created a hand-colored sample photograph for the colorist to follow.

title_tesim: Photograph of newspaper article, "L. A. Children Plan Golden Rule Week," 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1300, Bartlett, Adlebert [sic], nitrate
Handwritten at edge of negative: 2388 P 13
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Article includes photograph of McAdoo and 4 children.  Caption byline: Examiner photo.

Text reads: November 10, 1930.  L. A. Children Plan Golden Rule Week, McAdoo and Banker Address Small Folk at Meeting.  Children of all nationalities in Los Angeles yesterday began their preparations for the world observance of Golden Rule Week, December 7 to 14.  To spur them on were the words of William Gibbs McAdoo, wartime Cabinet member, and Dr. John Willis Baer, Pasadena banker, both of whom addressed a gathering of tiny folk at 1550 West Washington street.  The two men spoke briefly, explaining that the aim behind the coming week of observance is a desire to aid needy children in all lands, especially neglected parts of America.  Most of the children were dressed in native costume.  Among them were Mary Nakatsu, Japanese; Igore Lawb, Russian; Carlotta Chapman, Dutch, and Sarah Anjozian, Armenian.

title_tesim: Photograph of typewritten speech by William Gibbs McAdoo at the Golden Rule Foundation Pageant, 1930
caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2388 P17
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1300, Bartlett, Adelebert [sic], nitrate
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Text reads:  REMARKS BY HON. WILLIAM GIBBS McADOO AT THE GOLDEN RULE FOUNDATION PAGEANT, Saturday, November 8, 1930.  This is a visible demonstration of the universality of the appeal made by the greatest philanthropic organization in the world - THE GOLDEN RULE FOUNDATION.  I say the GREATEST because it is the only organization which devotes itself to the conservation and welfare of child life in every part of the world, without regard to race, class, religion or nationality.  The progress of civilization depends upon the health and vitality of the races that compose it, and no race can be strong or vital unless infant life is not only preserved, but surrounded by the care and conditions which produce vigorous and healthy types, mentally and physically.  In some countries half of the children who are born die in infancy.  This is an appalling fact and it is that condition which the GOLDEN RULE FOUNDATION is trying to relieve.  But its efforts will be impotent unless the children of the world, and those who stand back of them, help to raise the necessary money to carry on the work.  The GOLDEN RULE FOUNDATION undertakes to furnish medical assistance, food and clothing, and other things that are necessary to help the children of other countries who are less fortunate than those of the United States.  It is a noble mission and should have universal support.  Let us hope that the people of this great and powerful and wealthy nation will, during the coming campaign, which begins on Golden Rule Sunday, December 7th, and continues for an entire week, contribute generously to this great cause and enable the GOLDEN RULE FOUNDATION to provide for the destitute and under-privileged children in all nations of the world.  [signed] WGMcAdoo

description_tesim: Possibly connected to Los Angeles Times  article, August 9, 1932, Outline of Golden Rule Week Given, President of Foundation Tells Plan to Observe Period December 11-18.  Plans for the local observance of Golden Rule Week … were outlined yesterday by Charles V. Vickrey of New York, executive president of the Golden Rule Foundation … the meeting was in charge of Lincoln Wirt, Pacific Coast director of the foundation. … the public will be asked to deny themselves the extensive meals and other luxuries to which they have become accustomed, and donate the money saved in this way to needy and destitute children. …
Charles Vickrey and Lincoln Wirt, in suits and ties, standing, both holding check or small piece of paper, indoors with door in background

Plans for the local observance of Golden Rule Week … were outlined yesterday by Charles V. Vickrey of New York, executive president of the Golden Rule Foundation … the meeting was in charge of Lincoln Wirt, Pacific Coast director of the foundation. … the public will be asked to deny themselves the extensive meals and other luxuries to which they have become accustomed, and donate the money saved in this way to needy and destitute children. …

title_tesim: z - duplicate photograph - newspaper article, L.A. Children Plan Golden Rule Week, 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1300, Bartlett, Adlebert [sic], nitrate
Handwritten at edge of negative: 2388 P11
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Article includes photograph of McAdoo and 4 children. Caption byline: Examiner photo.

Text reads: Examiner.  November 10, 1930. L. A. Children Plan Golden Rule Week, McAdoo and Banker Address Small Folk at Meeting. Children of all nationalities in Los Angeles yesterday began their preparations for the world observance of Golden Rule Week, December 7 to 14. To spur them on were the words of William Gibbs McAdoo, wartime Cabinet member, and Dr. John Willis Baer, Pasadena banker, both of whom addressed a gathering of tiny folk at 1550 West Washington street. The two men spoke briefly, explaining that the aim behind the coming week of observance is a desire to aid needy children in all lands, especially neglected parts of America. Most of the children were dressed in native costume. Among them were Mary Nakatsu, Japanese; Igore Lawb, Russian; Carlotta Chapman, Dutch, and Sarah Anjozian, Armenian.

title_tesim: Horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made in 1888 by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: H 2957 File A, Ina Coolbrith, copied 4/30/40, and shell wreath she made in 1880's--Santa Monica, Poet Laureate of California 1914, John Crum
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made in 1888 by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith. The wreath was composed of sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

title_tesim: Karinova Ballet students performing in costume, Santa Monica, 1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Mme Karinova. 1958. 6/8/58.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Karinova Ballet students performing in costume. Three girls depict dolls set in boxes. They wear attire that mimics descriptions posted on each box: Chinese, Bride, and Ballerina. A boy in dress shirt and pants kneels with arms outstretched toward the ballerina, while a fourth girl, dressed in a night gown and holding a stuffed animal, lays curls up on the floor as if in bed. Three additional figures can be seen in the background on the left. They are not performing. Karinova School of Ballet, Deauville Beach Club, Santa Monica.

Dancers studied with Madame Karinova (aka Harriette Corinne Reinert Steinbeck) at the Karinova School of Ballet, Deauville Club at Santa Monica, as noted in the Ogden Standard–Examiner, 19 Sept. 1954: 25.
A notice for a performance by the Karinova Ballet at the Deauville Club appears in the Los Angeles Times, 17 Jun 1952: B4

title_tesim: “Carmen” production, Santa Monica
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in Act 1 of “Carmen.” A male cast member, likely portraying the soldier Don José, sings while a female cast member stands apart from him. Remaining male cast members are dressed either as soldiers or as typical villagers (short breeches with wide sashes, some wearing bandanas on their heads). The women wear dresses evoking Spanish attire- full skirts, shawls, hand fans, and flowers in their hair. The soldiers stand next to a scenery flat representing a guard house. Across the stage, a second flat represents a tobacco factory. A sign reading “Fabrica de Tobaco” hangs overhead. Photographed from the audience with view of the conductor and musicians in the orchestra pit, as well as audience members in the first few rows. This may possibly be a Santa Monica Civic Opera production. Stage is likely at a Santa Monica school auditorium. Date of production unknown.
No information on negative sleeve.

title_tesim: "Hansel and Gretel" production with June Moss, Barbara Ludwig and Charlie Marie Gordon (possibly), Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1959?
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1957-59. Hansel & Gretel
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Possibly related to a production of "Hansel and Gretel," January 23-24, 1959. The performance was conducted by Irving Beckman and performed at Barnum Hall. The notice appears in the Los Angeles Times, 18 Jan. 1959: E6

Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Hansel and Gretel." Cast members in German-style clothing form a line across the stage. Singers portraying young males wear lederhosen; the young women wear dirndls. June Moss (as the mother wearing a dark shawl) sings at left, while performers in the role of Hansel (possibly Charlie Marie Gordon) and Gretel (portrayed by Barbara Ludwig) flank their father (standing at center, holding a hat). Behind them, a stage set depicts a gingerbread cottage in the woods with cutouts of gingerbread children and a witch. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Dr. Cass Arthur Reed, Mrs. Albert L. Bagnall, and others, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2369 P1 to 9, 2370 P1 to 5, Near East Colleges, Dr. Cass A. Reed, Mrs. A.L. Bagnall, 951 Western, also Claremont -Pomona College.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Dr. Cass Arthur Reed was a graduate of Pomona College, and then later, Harvard, Columbia and the Union Theological Seminary. He earned degrees from all of these institutions. In 1912, he was appointed the Dean of the International College of Smyrna Turkey. He is referenced in numerous L.A. Times articles, including the Aug. 11, 1914 article "Pomona 'Grad' College Dean Home from Smyrna, Will Take Sports Leader with Him to Turkey".

This image is a group photo including Dr. Reed and likely Mrs. A.L. Bagnall, both standing to the right of the group. On the table before them are several booklets, one with the title "Asia". At the bottom of the picture is written "2369 P4".

description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.
Two people at Palisades Park standing in silhouette near a wooden rail fence and trees following the cliff side. Coastline with clouds and sun low over ocean in background.

title_tesim: View of ocean on Ocean Ave. and edge of cliffs, Santa Monica, circa 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: PH Philbin, 705 Ocean Street, Panor...(illegible).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building on the Pacific Coast Highway at the base of the palisades cliffs, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.

This image is of a sidewalk, street (likely Ocean Ave.) and across the street, Palisades Park in Santa Monica can be seen. Two cars can also be seen, one of which has a sign painted on that reads "Chas H. Fravel PLUMBING", in which a man sits in the driver seat.

title_tesim: View of cliffs of Palisades Park behind the Sorrento Beach Club, Santa Monica, 1952

description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.
This image is looking south on the cliffs of Palisades Park. In the lower right corner, part of the roof of the Sorrento Club can also be seen.

description_tesim: The Robert Major School of acting was located in Beverly Hills, as noted in directories from 1929 and 1930.

Four members of the Robert Majors School of Acting performing in a play. Two women and one man (holding a book in his hands) listen intently to a third woman standing at right. The setting is that of a kitchen in dwelling. Torn wall paper exposes the brick wall beneath. Curtained window are also visible. Additional set props include a light-colored hutch (on the far right), a bench, a linen-covered table and chairs.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.

title_tesim: Opera singer Julian Oliver and fellow singers (?) dressed as characters from “La Tosca,” Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2896 P 7
Text from negative sleeve: 2896 P 1-8. Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942. Fashion Show, Opera La Tosco [sic] 10/20/42. Julian Oliver 1725 N. Bronson, 1 set 8x10 DW Holly.

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph featuring tenor Julian Oliver with two performers, dressed as characters from the opera “La Tosca.” Oliver (posed at center wearing a buttoned overcoat with visible lace cuffs, and boots) is portraying the artist Mario Cavaradossi. At left, a man wearing a tailcoat, pants, shirt and white bow-tie rests one hand on Oliver’s (Cavaradossi’s) shoulder, gesturing towards him with the other hand. At right, a woman portraying Floria Tosca holds Oliver’s (Cavaradossi’s) hand. She wears a satin gown with embellished bodice and puffed sleeves, and a cascading train that pools onto the floor. They are posed on a stage or concrete platform. A wooden side table and stool are used as props. In the background, the room’s walls and space dividers are covered with tufted fabric. A functioning door (also covered with fabric) can be viewed on the right. Photographed in a room at the Del Mar Club, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Bird's-eye view of Santa Monica Beach and the Pacific Coast Highway, circa 1946
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2883 P45 to 56. Santa Monica. Palisades Park. Wide angle shots. 1939. 1950's. 3 young people up incline Calif st hill.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2883 P50
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: The original rustic branch fence at Palisades Park was gradually replaced with both a wood rail and a concrete fence. The project was completed around 1946. Here, both the fence along the Palisades path has been replaced, while portions of the rustic fence can be seen along the Idaho foot path. In addition, clothing styles and several automobiles in view suggests that the photo was taken circa mid-1940s.

View from the cliff edge at Palisades Park facing southward. From left to right: pedestrians viewed walking along the Palisades Park path, its replacement fence in view. Below, three people walk up the Idaho foot path; the California Incline is seen in the background and to the right, followed by Pacific Coast Highway, beach houses and beach below, with the pier in the distance.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: Ensemble photograph featuring tenor Julian Oliver with three performers, dressed as characters from the opera “La Tosca.” Oliver (second from left wearing a buttoned overcoat with visible lace cuffs, and boots) is portraying the artist Mario Cavaradossi.  He is being grasped by another performer in an officer’s uniform and boots. A woman portraying Floria Tosca holds Oliver’s (Cavaradossi’s) arm and looks off into the distance, while a third man (holding a bicorn hat and wearing a powdered wig, overcoat, waistcoat [vest], breaches and stockings) clenches her other wrist. He is likely portraying the Baron Scarpia. The performers are on a stage or concrete platform. Background walls are covered with tufted fabric. Photographed in a room at the Del Mar Club, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)

Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Bird's-eye view facing northwest from Palisades Park towards Marion Davies' house on Santa Monica Beach, with Will Rogers Beach visible beyond.

Photographs housed in this negative sleeve (labeled “P53-P56”) feature Marion Davies’ home. This photo shows the original rustic fence lining the path at Palisades Park. That fence was replaced around 1946. This photo was likely taken in 1939.

Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Man and woman dressed in Persian attire, Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2896 P 3
Text from negative sleeve: 2896 P 1-8. Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942. Fashion Show, Opera La Tosco [sic] 10/20/42. Julian Oliver 1725 N. Bronson, 1 set 8x10 DW Holly.
named_subject_tesim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Image of a couple dressed in Persian attire. He wears a bejeweled turban, long embroidered khalat (robe) over stripped sirwal (baggy trousers), and curled-toe slippers. She wears a shear veil partially covering her hair, harem pants and gold-tone sandals. He is perched on the edge of a bed covered with silken linens and pillows. She is seated on the floor beside him. They stare into each other’s eyes while he holds a large book in his hands (the pages inside appear to be sheet music). Set props include a swath of fabric pooled next to a pillow lying on the floor, and potted plants seen behind the couple. Photographed in a room at the Del Mar Club, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The cast, in period costume, take up the stage. Barbara Ludwig, in the role of Musetta, performs center-stage. A man likely portraying Alcindoro sits to her left and watches. The remaining characters watch from a table at stage left. A painted backdrop and set props recreate the Café Momus and adjoining buildings. The exterior set also includes a table and chairs (the table set with bottles and glasses), hanging lanterns, and a poster advertising “livres-musique.” Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge; the entire stage, orchestra pit and rows of audience members viewed from balcony. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The cast, in period costume, take up the stage, while the main protagonists (Marcello, Mimi, Rodolfo, Colline and Schaunard) sit at a table stage left. A painted backdrop and set props recreate the Café Momus and adjoining buildings. The exterior set also includes a table and chairs (the table set with bottles and glasses), hanging lanterns, and a poster advertising “livres-musique.” Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while a portion of the orchestra pit and four audience members are seen at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” scene with Natalie Garrotto, Ray Gagan and Enrico Porta performed during "One Night at the Opera," Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1950
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Nite [sic] of opera. 163 to 170.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 166
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing Act 2, Scene 2 from “Lucia di Lammermoor.” At center stage stands Enrico, portrayed by Enrico Porta, flanked by Edgardo (Raymond Gagan at left in tall boots, fitted breeches and shirt) and Raimondo (Richard Kissler). Enrico and Raimondo both wear satin doublets with high collars and breeches with stockings. Lucia (Natalie Garrotto) stands off to their right. She wears a wedding gown and veil. She is flanked by her betrothed, Arturo (Bruce Wescott wearing a dark-colored doublet, breeches and high collar), and her handmaid, Alisa (Catherine Mazet). Cast members (including June Moss) portray wedding attendees who watch as events transpire. The setting is a hall in Lammermoor Castle. Upstage, a stair case and narrow wall panels suggest a grand room. Downstage, two chairs and a table topped with quill pen and ink are the only props visible.

Excerpt from “Lucia di Lammermoor” was performed as part of the Santa Monica Civic Opera's “One Night at the Opera,” an event showcasing scenes from the association’s most popular operas. The performance took place January 28, 1950 at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Opera singer Natalie Garrotto in the title role, performing the “Mad Scene” from “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Garrotto stands center stage while cast members, dressed as 17th century nobles, watch events unfold. The setting is a hall in Lammermoor Castle. Upstage, a stair case and narrow wall panels suggest a grand room. Downstage, two chairs and a table topped with quill pen and ink are the only props visible. Musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: “Rigoletto” production,  John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 22 Rigoletto
Text from negative sleeve: Rigaletto. 9/22/51. Lewis Ma...
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “Rigoletto.” The singers, portraying nobles and courtiers, wear costumes evoking 16th century attire - the female singers wear long gowns with full skirts and puffed sleeves. The men wear stockings, doublets with paneled sleeves, ruff collars, and baldrics (for holding a sword) across their chests. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace, replete with paneled walls set with Corinthian inspired columns and a frieze over a curtained passageway. A painting hangs on a wall behind a throne - here represented by an ornate wooden chair; a chandelier hangs overhead. Musicians viewed from orchestra pit at foreground. Additionally, June Moss (far left) performs as a noble in attendance.
This performance of “Rigoletto” was possibly presented at John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Text reads, in part: Looking Back 25 Years.  ... Three days after the races the names of the winning car drivers were announced in the Outlook, when the prize money was paid to them.  Durant was first and received $4000; Hearne, second, won $2500; Le Cocq, third, $1500, and Pullen, fourth, $1000.  Durant's average speed was 81.88 miles an hour for the 250 miles of the course. title_tesim: "Pagliacci" production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 23
Text from negative sleeve: [?] to 25. SM Opera. Cavalleria & Pagliacci. 1955. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Pagliacci, a drama in two acts, was performed in a double-bill with Pietro Mascagni’s opera, Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act.

Opera singers in a production of Ruggero Leoncavallo’s, Pagliacci, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera. Children from the audience sit onstage with cast members, making up the audience for a play-within-a-play. They watch as Nedda (playing Columbina) is serenated by her lover Harlequin.  She stands on a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple. The stage is covered by a canopy with drapery; a drum is propped against the frame. A banner painted with “I Pagliacci” is attached to the pediment. Musicians in the orchestra pit can be seen in the immediate foreground. Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7

Singers portraying Lola and Turiddu stand slightly to the fore of the group; most of the players turn their attention towards him. Behind the players, an Italian village is depicted using a painted backdrop and set replete with a wine shop and church. The conductor and musicians are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. title_tesim: Santa Monica High School campus building entryway, Santa Monica, 1939

named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: Photograph shows a second floor campus building entrance, seen from the vantage point of a patio or rest area with benches and drinking fountains. Following the path from the covered entry, a sculptural work called “The Viking” stands between two sets of windows on the left. The pathway continues to an additional entry and, likely, to the adjacent building. The curved walkway follows the same lines as the first-floor windows, partially seen from this view. Photographed at Santa Monica High School.
Portions of Santa Monica High School were destroyed during the earthquake of 1933. Its reconstruction fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1964. Ballet La Canada. Music Arts.
Handwritten on photograph: Constantine
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The caption on the photograph, "Constantine," refers to the photographer Constantine Hassalevris. When an automobile accident ended his career as a professional dancer, Hassalevris became a photographer. He photographed Danilova, Dolin, Eglevsky, Lichine, Markova, Massine, Slavenska, Tudor, Toumanova and Zorina, among others and was later the photographer for Dance magazine.

Andrea Karlsen, a native of Los Angeles, began her dance studies in New Zealand with Estelle Berre, then studied in Los Angeles with Bronislava Nijinska. After WWII, Nijinska took her to France to join Le Grand Ballets du Marquis de Cuevas, where she attained principal dancer status in 1953. She remained with the company until 1955. She and her husband, the dancer Paul Maure, founded  the Ballet des Arts Company in La Canada in 1957.
Photograph of ballet dancer Andrea Karlsen posing in a romantic style tutu by the photographer Constantine.

title_tesim: Man feeding chickens outside of a coop, Fontana, 1928-1931
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2098… 2102–2103–2104–2105–2106–2107–2108–2109–2110–2111–2112. Fontana. Chickens – Corrals. [stamped:] Adelbert Bartlett Los Angeles
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2111
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Fontana was founded in 1913 by A.B. (Azarial Blanchard) Miller, d. 1878-1941. Miller first purchased the 17,000 acres of land in 1906, a year after he began working it. Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. Miller, Fontana Farms and Fontana Land companies from 1914 to 1937; articles specific to poultry farming and egg production date from 1925 to 1931.

Unidentified man feeding a flock of Leghorn chickens outside of a large coop. The coop is wood-sided with a shingled roof; chicken wire covers the open windows. The man holds a feed basket and wears a dress shirt, slacks and an ivy cap.  Photographed in Fontana.

title_tesim: “Marriage of Figaro” production with Lou Ann Wyckoff, June Moss and Caterina Micieli, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 3/22/58. Figaro.
named_subject_tesim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in Act 2 of  a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “The Marriage of Figaro.” Cherubino (portrayed by female singer Lou Ann Wyckoff) sings at center stage while Countess Almaviva (June Moss) and Susanna (Caterina Micieli with guitar) look on. Cherubino and Countess Almaviva wear 18th century attire – he in a long coat, vest (waistcoat) and breeches, she in a lace gown and lace-trimmed dressing robe. The countess also wears a bouffant wig. Susanna, her servant, wears a full skirt and peasant blouse topped with a bodice. The setting is one of the Countess’ rooms in Count Almaviva’s palace, with ornately painted walls and a curtain balcony that becomes the room’s focal point. Set props are end tables and chairs. Musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.  Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro

title_tesim: Cast members Judith Evans and Stanley Herbert Briggs from “O.K. By Me,” directed by Louis Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Actors Judith Evans and Stanley Herbert Briggs, from the production “O.K. By Me,” photographed on stage.

Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

title_tesim: Nelda Scarsella and Gene Curtsinger performing in “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 27
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 1. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta (wearing a tiered gown with lace trim). She sings at center with Gene Curtsinger (in the role of Alfredo Germont). An unidentified performer portraying Baron Douphel stands at far left with arms crossed and back turned from the revelry. Cast members, wearing gowns and coattails, raise their champagne glasses and sing as a chorus along with Violetta and Alfredo. The setting is a salon in Violetta’s Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including upholstered chairs and settee, and a chandelier hanging above a table set with a lace table cloth. Beyond the stage, a portion of the string section can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 3. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta. She wears a white dressing gown embellished with feathers. She is held by Gene Curtsinger, in the role of Alfredo Germont; Enrico Porta, portraying Giorgio Germont, approaches from the right. An additional male singer portraying Dr. Grenvil and an unidentified singer in the role of Annina, Violetta’s maid, stand behind her. The setting is Violetta’s bedroom in her Parisian home. Props include a bed, lace covered nightstand, side table, chairs and a settee. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit beyond the stage at foreground.

title_tesim: Cast members of “O.K. By Me” with director Louise Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

Cast members of "O.K. by Me," with director Louise Glaum, seen at left holding what appears to be a playbill. Cast includes Peggy O'Connell (standing right of Glaum), Pearle Jennings (in blackface, holding a whisk broom), and Judith Evans.

title_tesim: Marker for the end of the Kearney Trail with the Cosmopolitan Hotel in the background, San Diego, 1930-1945
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2805. San Diego. Old Town historical sites. Bowers museum. Juan Bandini. Old Town.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Cosmopolitan Hotel is located at the southeast corner of the plaza in Old Town San Diego Historic Park. Originally the Casa de Bandini, it was constructed in 1827-1829 as a single-story adobe residence. It became a hotel in 1869 when a wood frame 2nd floor was added, and was an olive factory from 1900-1919. This photograph was taken sometime after the 1930 remodel in the steamboat revival style popular in Louisiana with vertical lath screens along the top of the porch and balcony. James and Nora Cardwell, who purchased the building in 1945, remodeled it in the Spanish Colonial style. Its  restoration to its 1872 state, as the Cosmopolitan Hotel, was completed around 2010.

View of the Cosmopolitan Hotel (Casa de Bandini) in Old Town San Diego State Historic Park when it was owned by Cave Couts, Jr. In the foreground is a man standing in front of the marker for the end of the Kearney Trail (Southern Emigrant Trail), placed by the Daughters of the American Revolution in 1920.

title_tesim: Ada E. Bowers Memorial Fountain in the courtyard of the Bowers Museum, Santa Ana, between 1936-1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2805. San Diego. Old Town historical sites. Bowers museum. Juan Bandini. Old Town.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: In March of 1931 the Santa Ana City Council accepted the offer of a $100,000 museum and site established by Mr. and Mrs. Charles W. Bowers. The  museum  was completed in September 1932 and opened in 1936.

View of the Ada E. Bowers Memorial Fountain in the courtyard of the Bowers Museum, with a statue of Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo, discoverer of California, and bas-relief scenes behind the statue and along the base of the fountain. To the left of Cabrillo is a rocky formation from which water issues. Behind Cabrillo are 3 scenes including Cabrillo and companions facing Indians on the sea shore with a ship in the ocean in the distance, a mission church, and Indians in a wooden carat (carretta) drawn by oxen. At the base are 8 bas-relief scenes apparently depicting episodes from the mission era and later settlement by ranchers and farmers. A plaque in the center bears the text "Mrs. Bowers Memorial Fountain."

title_tesim: Pioneer Society Picnic, Santa Monica, 1940 or 1944
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2933. Santa Monica. Pioneer Society Picnic, Santa Monica, 8/15/1940 and 1944. Frank Bundy, Chas. H Tegner etc. 500 years …
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The annual picnic was established by the Santa Monica Pioneer Society in 1909 as an event where long-time residents could gather and “catch up” on each other’s lives. It became a venue where those who helped build the community would be recognized.

An audience of men, women and children attending the Pioneer Society Picnic. Rows of benches have been set up amidst palm trees in a neighborhood park. The seated crowd is not focused on anyone or thing in particular. Most appear to be in conversation with their companions. In the background, neighborhood homes are seen across the tree-lined street, with both parked and moving cars in view.

title_tesim: View of Pacific Palisades and coast from Miramar Estates, Pacific Palisades, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: add to earlies. Pacific Palisades from Miramar Estates, 1952.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Lake Shrine (viewed in the image, lower right) sits on 10-acres of property owned by the Self Realization Fellowship, acquired 1950.

View of Pacific Palisades and coast from Miramar housing development. The photograph captures both the undeveloped and developed landscape. Atop the hill at left stands a large home ensconced by trees. On the opposite side of the valley stands another hilltop structure. The dwelling overlooks a paved road that follows the coastline and curves around a lake. The lake’s visible features include a lotus-inspired shrine, a Dutch windmill, and an additional unidentified object (opposing the shrine). In the background, the Pacific Palisades community and Pacific Ocean extend into the distance.

subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: Beachgoers relax on the sands in front of a row of beach houses. The houses sit behind walls and fences along the beach with the Palisades Park cliffs in the background.

A rustic branch fence can be seen running along the Palisades Park cliffs. Here, a portion of the fence above the third and fourth homes from the right shows signs of repair. The branch fence was eventually replaced with a wood rail fence around 1946.

title_tesim: Eugene d'Orange telling Valeska Radd and June Diebold their fortunes, Santa Monica, 1931
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2508 P 2
Text from negative sleeve: 2511. Santa Monica. Eugene d'Orange. Eugene Drake, 1931. "Ancient" writing? See story.

Text from related typescript document: Valeska Radd, Yugo-Slavian blond Beauty… seated, left) with June Diebold, University of California co-ed, having their “fortune” told by Eugene d’Orange, Scotch-Spanish poet and artist of Santa Monica, California, who upon his return from a journey to the Levant, wears Arab head-dress as protection from the sun and is introducing a strange Oriental writing in a tray of sand which he interprets in answer to those seeking advice in love, “always an easy and favorite subject with me,” sayd d’Orange, who is the author of poems of passion.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of Eugene d’Orange, wearing a keffiyeh and agal, with Valeska Radd (at left, also wearing a keffiyeh) and June Diebold. Eugene d’Orange is viewed writing in a tray of sand. Both women wear similar bathing suits with related patches on the chest. Miss Diebold’s patch reads: “A Roger C[ornell] Athlete.” The trio is seated on the sands of a Santa Monica Beach, with additional beachgoers and the coastline on view in background.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performers singing carols in a Christmas-themed photomontage, 1955
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1955. Santa Monica Opera Xmas Cards. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photomontage image, possibly intended for use as a Christmas card. The photograph features members of the Santa Monica Civic Opera dressed in Dickens-style costumes, singing from books of Christmas carols. This image has been superimposed onto a photograph of an unidentified, 2-story home with an outdoor Christmas tree. In addition, a brick wall has been added between the carolers and the property, creating a relatively seamless composite. Civic opera singers include: Barbara Ludwig (front row, second from left), June Moss (center), Teresa Kirbe and Enrico Porta (in top hat, holding a small dog). Caption at top right: "To wish you happiness not only at Christmas but always."

The photograph of Santa Monica Civic Opera singers dressed as Dickens carolers was taken at the Bullock's department store in Westwood. The house that was used as a backdrop is unidentified. (See image ark nos. 21198/zz002d6dkb and 21198/zz002d6dnc.)

description_tesim: Note from opera singer June Moss to photographer Adelbert Bartlett about photographs he took of her in her Countess Almaviva costume for her performance in the Santa Monica Civic Opera production of the  Marriage of Figaro.

Note reads: 3-24. Mr. B: Enclosed is Times article - you may keep it. Thanks for the oratorio tickets - I hope to attend. I agree tickets are best. Who-ever handles publicity next time should have samples. Bob Mahoney seems to be the best bet for the job. Had you spoken to him? I tho't Dr. Gordon's criticism very good, albeit too lengthy. It should be shortened if submitted to any publication, I feel. I've noted on reverse of proofs what I'd like - will have money next week. June

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 30
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. Singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) stands center stage. She and the other female performers (including Natalie Garrotto portraying Natalie (Valencienne), seated at the table on the left) wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country, including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Cast members stand and behind her as she sings. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee. Photographed from the balcony, providing a view of the stage and musicians in the orchestra pit.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. A singer portraying Prince Danilo (Count Danilo) performs at center stage. At left stands a singer portraying the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari). Additional cast members, dressed as party guests, look on. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. The women wear evening gowns; the men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt, waistcoat, breaches and stockings. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Visible stage props include chairs and a settee. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

description_tesim: View of the Ah Louis Store at 800 Palm St. on a corner next to a row of wooden buildings.

On Wong (1840 – December 16, 1936), more commonly known as Ah Louis, was a Chinese American banker, labor contractor, farmer, and shopkeeper in San Luis Obispo, California during the late 19th and early 20th century. His Ah Louis Store building is on the National Register of Historic Places.

title_tesim: Photograph of a painting by James Fagan, [painting, 1890]
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2980. San Jose, Calif. Styles of 1880's.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Copy print photograph of an illustration or hand colored etching by James (Jas) Fagan, signed and dated 1890. The painting depicts a park scene with two women, seated at a picnic table, in conversation with a man wearing a three-piece suit, tie and bowler hat. A young girl stands next to him. Children are viewed playing kick ball on the lawn behind them, while small child and its parents are viewed at far right. Finally, a female figure appears to be stepping into a horse drawn carriage, the door being held open by a valet or carriage driver (far right, background).

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 35
Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.

description_tesim: “L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  October 13 and November 3, 1951.

Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “L’Elisir d’amore.” The performers, mostly women with two men, are dressed in peasant attire. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone structures including a cantina complete with a table and chair. Performers include Natalie Garrotto (seated second from left), June Moss (singing, near center), and Kay Marshall (L of man seated at right). Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” June Moss, portraying Adina, sings with a male performer in the role of Nemorino. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century, reflected in the painted backdrop depicting rolling hills and trees, and additional set pieces painted to mimic stone architecture. Musicians can be viewed from the orchestra pit at forefront. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

Photograph of Roger Hansen in costume as Dr. Dulcamara for a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “L’Elisir d’amore.” He wears a top hat with peacock feather, cape and coat, waist coat (vest), striped cravat and sash, trousers and boots. He also holds a walking stick in one hand, a bottle of elixir in the other. A leather bag rest at his feet. Photographed in a hallway at John Adams Auditorium.

description_tesim: Opera singers performing in a production of “Cavalleria Rusticana.” Cast members wearing Italian peasant costumes are gathered center stage while a male singer performing as Alfio stands to the fore of the group, holding a whip. The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. At opposing ends of the stage are a cantina and a church. Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit, while audience members are viewed at foreground. Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Image housed with other negatives from a performance of “Cavalleria Rusticana” at Barnum Hall, May 11, 1952. This is a different performance at the same venue with similar sets. This performance may have been staged the same year.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “Pagliacci.” The stage is filled with cast members in peasant costumes, as performers portraying Canio/ Pagliacci (in clown costume) and Canio’s wife Nedda ride onstage on a horse drawn wagon. The setting is a 19th century Calabrian village. With rolling hills and trees painted on a backdrop. To the fore stands a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple. Drawn curtains shield the interior from view. A banner painted with “I Pagliacci” is attached to the pediment. Beyond the stage, two musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit. Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Page of article regarding the one hundredth anniversary of the Pony Express postal service, 1960-1964
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: B. Herrick - John Crum. Spur.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph copy of an article page regarding the 100th anniversary of the Pony Express postal service. The page has been photographed or cropped to capture the top half of the page which includes images with descriptive captions. At top, an illustration of a man wearing western attire riding on horseback. He is being pursued by Native Americans (also on horseback). The accompanying caption reads: "Hostile Indians were a constant hazard for riders - as caught here by the Western artist, Herman W. Hansen." Below left, a portrait of Alexander Majors, one of the Pony Express founders. Caption reads: "A. Majors: Lay preacher best remembered for his experiment that failed." Remaining text reads: "April, 1960, Marks the 100th Anniversary of the Pony Express from...St. Joe to Sacramento."

Negative sleeve information does not match photograph. This reprinted article matches one housed in another negative sleeve. Text from negative sleeve: Pony Express, 1860-1960. 1964 ME Church Magazine, Chicago. (For associated image, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d7f10.)
A date of 1964 is noted on the negative sleeve. It is possibly a reprint date. The complete article was written in 1960 (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d781k).

title_tesim: Clipping regarding an interview with performer Josephine Baker, written by Art Buchwald, 1960
caption_tesim: Text form negative sleeve: 1950s. From Paris, Jos. Baker.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Art Buckwald's column was published in numerous newspapers. This particular article appeared in the New London (Connecticut) paper, The Evening Day, 16 Jan. 1960:4.

Newspaper clipping of a column written by Art Buchwald (1925-2007) titled, "Josephine Baker is not Angry." In the interview from Paris, Buchwald speaks with the then 54-year-old expatriate performer about her plans to return to the United States. Baker planned to bring her revue to Florida and Chicago before ending on Broadway. The article notes Baker's support of 11 adopted children as the reason for her return. She also addresses the misquote that led the public to believe she would never come back to America, and her famous public battle with columnist and former ally Walter Winchell.

title_tesim: Louis B. Mayer Memorial Bench dedication ceremony at Palisades Park, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day, 1937. Santa Monica Dedication Park Seat. Mayor Gillett. Eldred Meyers Head of Native Sons.
named_subject_tesim: Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is one of five photographs documenting the ceremony that took place at Palisades Park. The Georgian Hotel (located at 1415 Ocean Ave.) is visible in the background of one photo (image ark no. 21198/zz002d7g9m).
This dedication ceremony marked the official start of Admissions Day festivities and is noted in the article, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the Admission Day celebration, a three-day festival (September 8-10) observing California's admission to the Union. The celebration was presented by the Native Sons and Native Daughters of the Golden West.

Participants in the Louis B. Mayer Memorial Bench dedication ceremony at Palisades Park. Mayor Edmond Gillete is photographed at center. To his right stands Eldred L. Meyer, Grand President of the Native Sons of the Golden West, followed by actor Charles Grapwin. The flag bearers (holding American and California flags) are probably Native Son members. The group stands behind the memorial bench affixed with two plaques. Text from upper plaque reads: "Dedicated by the Native Sons of the Golden West September 8 1937." Text from lower plaque: "The Louis B. Mayer Memorial Seat. To the Pioneers of Motion Pictures. Dedicated to the Pioneer Actors, Producers, Directors, and Technicians who in the first decade of the Twentieth Century laid in Santa Monica the foundation of the Motion Picture Industry in Western America....."
combined_subject_ssim: Native Sons of the Golden West
