title_tesim: Actor William S. Hart and son (possibly) on horseback in the California Admission Day Parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
named_subject_tesim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

Photograph of two men and a teen-aged boy, wearing western attire, riding on horseback in the California Admission Day Parade. The man at center may be the actor William S. Hart. The boy at left, William S. Hart, Jr. The men and boy raise their cowboy hats as they look towards the camera. They are followed by horse drawn carriages driven by men who also wear western clothing. They are following the second half of the parade route along Ocean Ave., south of Santa Monica Blvd. The tall building in the background at left is the Georgian Hotel.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West

title_tesim: Partial page of article regarding the one hundredth anniversary of the Pony Express postal service, 1960-1964
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Pony Express, 1860-1960. 1964 ME Church Magazine, Chicago.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph copy of an article page regarding the 100th anniversary of the Pony Express postal service. The page has been photographed or cropped to capture the top half of the page which includes images with descriptive captions. At top, an illustration of a man wearing western attire riding on horseback. He is being pursued by Native Americans (also on horseback). The accompanying caption reads: "Hostile Indians were a constant hazard for riders - as caught here by the Western artist, Herman W. Hansen." Below left, a portrait of Alexander Majors, one of the Pony Express founders. Caption reads: "A. Majors: Lay preacher best remembered for his experiment that failed." Remaining text reads: "April, 1960, Marks the 100th Anniversary of the Pony Express from...St. Joe to Sacramento."
A date of 1964 is noted on the negative sleeve. It is possibly a reprint date. The complete article associated with this image was written in 1960 (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d781k).

title_tesim: Clipping regarding racial prejudice and (possibly) the voting rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama, 1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: C.S. Boston. 3/26/65. Children.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a partial version of a full page article (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d7f31).

Photograph of an article titled, "Many March Against Mankind's Prejudices," Christian Science Monitor, 26 Mar. 1965. The article's legible text recounts a story of two Chicago children of different races, framing the story within the context of a contemporaneous Supreme Court case that challenged the constitutionality of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Interviews with school-aged children are included, as are interviews with adults (partially legible here). Changing attitudes in regards to race, as well as individual rights in relation to the rights of all citizens, appear to be the article's overarching themes. The article's title may refer to the Selma to Montgomery voting rights march, which took place March 21, 1965.
Article published March 26, 1965. Reprint date unknown.

title_tesim: View toward lighthouse building from Palisades cliffs above Will Rogers Beach, Los Angeles, 1930s
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2890 P 1-3. Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades. Santa Monica area. Will Rogers State Beach Lighthouse & mountains. Best is 2890 P 1, for commercial sales – enlarging
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2890 P 3
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph view facing north toward the Santa Monica Bay and lighthouse building on Will Rogers State Beach, taken atop a Palisades cliff. A portion of Pacific Coast Highway is seen bottom left; a cactus grows with shrubbery on the cliffs at right.
The lighthouse building was purchased by Will Rogers in 1928 and eventually demolished in 1978. It was not a functioning lighthouse. Co-ordinates derived from original lighthouse location.

One of a series of photographs labeled 2890 P 1-5. All were shot from the same cliff toward the lighthouse building. Images labeled “2890 P 1-3” look to be from the 1930s. The building displays minimal signage and 1930s era automobiles are in view. Images labeled “2890 P 4-5” include 1940s era cars, while the building itself has accumulated more signage and is most similar to an additional photograph (No. 2902) of Caroline Bartlett taken at the same location, dated 1946.
combined_subject_ssim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)

The boy on the right holds a slice of bread lifted from a stack of bread slices on a plate on the table in front of him next to a loaf of bread and a knife. The label on the loaf of bread reads "Honey Kurl Wheat Bread," and the top of the jam jar is labeled "Kraft..."

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

title_tesim: Clipping regarding racial prejudice and (possibly) the voting rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama, 1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: C.S. Boston. 3/26/65. Children.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: For a slightly more legible copy of this article, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d7f2h.

Photograph of an article titled, "Many March Against Mankind's Prejudices," Christian Science Monitor, 26 Mar. 1965. The article's legible text recounts a story of two Chicago children of different races, framing the story within the context of a contemporaneous Supreme Court case that challenged the constitutionality of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Interviews with school-aged children are included, as are interviews with adults (partially legible here). Changing attitudes in regards to race, as well as individual rights in relation to the rights of all citizens, appear to be the article's overarching themes. The article's title may refer to the Selma to Montgomery voting rights march, which took place March 21, 1965.
Article published March 26, 1965. Reprint date unknown.

title_tesim: Canadian Legion parade on Santa Monica Blvd. at 4th St., Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 3
Text from negative sleeve: 2815. #1 to #10 inc. Canadian Legion, 7/5/37. Santa Monica Parade. City of Santa Monica.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d7pj4 (document)
ark:/21198/zz002d7pkn (document)
description_tesim: Photograph of Canadian Legionnaires  marching in the seventh annual California State convention of the Canadian Legion, British Empire Service League. The men heading up the line wear light-colored pants and shirts with bow-ties, sashes across their chests, and garrison caps. Several wear medals on their chests. They are followed by flag bearers carrying both American and Union flags. Some of the flag bearers wear traditional Scottish highland attire. The procession follows street car tracks along Santa Monica Boulevard past Fourth Street, passing in front of a row of businesses. Signs on buildings and awnings read: "[Wom]en's Wear," "Ye Art Needlework Shop," "[Typ]ewriters Rented, Repaired," "...and Adding Machines," and "Hardware." A two-story building housing a Southern Pacific ticket office and a dentist office amongst other businesses. Finally, the four-story H.C. Henshey Co. department store is viewed on the far end of the block (at Fourth Street).

The event was covered in a Los Times article, "Legionnaire Conclave Climaxed by Parade: Canadian Veterans March 1500 Strong Through Streets of Santa Monica," 6 Jul. 1937: A17. Also, the Bartlett collection includes related clippings from a Santa Monica newspaper. The 2-page article is titled, "Legion Parade Today Feature of Convention," 5 Jul. 1937. (see "Relation" note).

ark:/21198/zz002d7pj4 (document)
ark:/21198/zz002d7pkn (document)
description_tesim: Photograph of a Navy band marching in the seventh annual California State convention of the Canadian Legion, British Empire Service League. The men wear sailor uniforms, including sailor caps and spats (leggings). They are led by a drum major seen at center. An officer in dress uniform marches at left. The group is followed by uniformed men (likely Royal Canadian Navy) holding rifles over their left shoulders. The lead soldier holds a saber that rests against his right shoulder. The procession follows street car tracks along Santa Monica Boulevard near Third Street, with the Junipher building seen at right. The building housed an S.H. Kress & Co. 5-10-25 Cent Store, a Union Pacific ticket office, a jeweler and a See's Candy store amongst other businesses. The Guaranty Trust Bank Building (221-225 Santa Monica Blvd.) can be seen farther down the street.

The event was covered in a Los Times article, "Legionnaire Conclave Climaxed by Parade: Canadian Veterans March 1500 Strong Through Streets of Santa Monica," 6 Jul. 1937: A17. Also, the Bartlett collection includes related clippings from a Santa Monica newspaper. The 2-page article is titled, "Legion Parade Today Feature of Convention," 5 Jul. 1937. (Image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7pj4 and 21198/zz002d7pkn)

title_tesim: Stern of the "Mon Lei" Chinese junk, Newport, 1944
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2866. Balboa. Old ship. etc. 1944.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View of the stern of the Mon Lei, a Chinese junk, anchored at Newport Bay. A relief panel presents affronted lions on  clouds. Below the panel is the ship's name and place of manufacture in roman script and, apparently, in Chinese characters: "Mon Lei," "Aberdeen." On the left, a sign reads: "On Exhibition, Adults 15¢, Children 10¢." On the right are 4 men on a diving platform with a 5th diver in the air.

The "Mon Lei" was a three-masted, gaff-rigged Chinese junk with balanced lug sails. The hull was built of teak and other Chinese hardwoods. It was 50' long, 18' wide, and had a draft of 6.5 feet. It was built in Aberdeen, Hong Kong.

description_tesim: El Mirasol began as a mansion built by Mary Herter, the mother of the artist Albert Herter. After his mother's death, Herter renovated the mansion and converted it into a boutique hotel. The hotel was purchased by Charles B. Hervey from John B. Quigley in 1941. The city block site of the former El Mirasol Hotel was dedicated as Alice Keck Park in 1980. The block is bounded by Santa Barbara, Micheltorena, Garden, and Arrellaga Streets.

Photograph of Charles B. Hervey at the entrance to El Mirasol Hotel shortly after he became the owner. He extends a hand in greeting. Hervey stands in a monumental doorway framed by carved acanthus leaves and with an iron grille.

description_tesim: The Hotel San Diego was developed by John D. Spreckles, designed by architect Harrison Albright in the "Chicago style"  and constructed in 1914, in anticipation of the Panama California International Exposition in 1915-1916. It was located at 339 West Broadway and was designated as San Diego Historical Site No. 175 in 1983. It was demolished in April 2006.

title_tesim: Mayor Claude C. Crawford, Chief Webb and others in the reviewing stand at the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration parade, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2901

Text from negative sleeve: 2901. Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. July 27/1940 in Santa Monica. Street Parade. Elks float. "Death Valley" Scotty. Mayor Claude Crawford, -- Johnson, Scotty's partner in group photo; Don Jackson, Carolyn Bartlett. 3: Dr Claude C Crawford U.S.C., Earl T Crawford, Barker Bros, Claude Crawford, mayor of Santa Monica.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School. The parade travelled from Colorado and 4th, to Wilshire Blvd., to Ocean Ave., to Pico, and then Main St. to City Hall.

Photograph of Mayor Claude C. Crawford and others seated in a reviewing stand, watching the "Roundup of the Stars"  parade - part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. The Mayor is seen with one arm raised. He is seated next to Police Chief Clarence E. Webb (in uniform).  Above the stands flies a banner which reads, in part: "Will Rogers Highway. Santa Monica July 25, 26, 27." The large tree in the background suggests that the location might be Palisades Park.

Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940: A1.

title_tesim: Carolyn Bartlett with actor Gilbert Roland at the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration parade, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2901

Text from negative sleeve: 2901. Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. July 27/1940 in Santa Monica. Street Parade. Elks float. "Death Valley" Scotty. Mayor Claude Crawford, -- Johnson, Scotty's partner in group photo; Don Jackson, Carolyn Bartlett. 3: Dr Claude C Crawford U.S.C., Earl T Crawford, Barker Bros, Claude Crawford, mayor of Santa Monica.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Carolyn Bartlett with Mexican-born actor Gilbert Roland on horseback at the "Roundup of the Stars" street parade, part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. Roland is seated in the saddle on a bridled horse and wears vaquero attire - a short jacket, trousers with two stripes down the side, a gun belt, and spurs on his boots. Carolyn Bartlett wears a dress and sport jacket. They are photographed on a Santa Monica street with homes on view in the background. Additional horses can be seen processing along the parade route behind Carolyn.

Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940: A1.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

title_tesim: Mayor Claude C. Crawford, conscripted men and other participants in the Army draft ceremony, Santa Monica, 1940

description_tesim: Photograph of Mayor Claude C. Crawford (at far left) standing on the steps of Santa Monica City Hall with ten conscripted men and other participants in an Army draft ceremony. The men were some of the first to be drafted under the Selective Service Act. Participants include city officials and men in uniform - two of whom hold American flags. The Santa Monica Bay Cities American Legion flag, and a Veterans Service League flag are viewed in the background.

Newspaper Clipping of an article by Herbert Donalson titled: 'Magic Flute' at The Shrine Has Great Beauty and Color'. The article is a review of a production of Mozart's opera "The Magic Flute" in English by the Guild Opera Company. The article praises highly Kay Marshall who played the role of Queen of the Night, Ken Remo as Tamino, and John Barnett, the conductor.

title_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall, Santa Monica, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, New City Hall Building itself. 12/1/39. "Dedication" [struck out].
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a view of the historic Santa Monica City Hall looking north-east from the lawns on the city hall grounds. Two women sit on the grass in the foreground while a group of people gather at the entrance. A bicycle rests on the steps leading up to th entrance. Santa Monica city hall has been considered one of the best examples of the Public Works Administration (PWA) Moderne style of architecture, made popular by depression-era architects. This style is a combination of the Art deco and Art Moderne styles, most often found in buildings financed by the federal government.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: This is a view of the historic Santa Monica City Hall looking east from the lawns on the side of the driveway leading up to it. The words "City Hall" can be seen etched over the grand entrance. Santa Monica City Hall is considered one of the best examples of the Public Works Administration (PWA) Moderne style of architecture, popularized by depression-era architects. This style is a combination of the Art deco and Art moderne styles, most often found in buildings financed by the federal government.

title_tesim: Man diving into El Mirador hotel pool, Palm Springs, 1941
caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: No. 2761-P410-421, El Mirador Hotel, Palm Springs, Pool - 2/1/41
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a picture taken of the historic El Mirador Hotel in Palm Springs. It was regarded one of the finest luxury hotels of its time. This is a picture of its famous Olympic-size swimming pool that had five diving boards, an underwater window on the pool for use by photographers, and a pool observation platform. A man dives from one of the boards, as people on the deck watch him. In the background, we can see the most defining feature of the hotel, a pink Byzantine tower from which the hotel took its name — El Mirador — meaning "The Lookout."

S. flag, enthroned before a sun medallion with the message "Palm Springs enjoys the Freedom of the Desert" and palm fronds at the base. The float platform is slip-covered in fabric with a printed scalloped hem and is towed by an automobile just visible in the lower left corner of the image.

title_tesim: Chuckawalla Slim seated beside his mineral and curio shop housed in a trailer, Palm Springs vicinity, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761 P255-258. Palm Springs. Indian Reserve. Palms. Chapel etc. Bath House & Vicinity. 1934.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: View of Chuckawalla Slim a travelling mineral and curio salesman, seated in a director's chair holding a mineral specimen, beside a trailer which served as his shop. He is shirtless and sits facing away from the photographer, probably to exhibit tattoos in his arm and back. Signs on the trailer read  "The Rockologist, Chuckwalla Slim," "Mineralogical Society of So. Calif.," "We Sell Rare Crystals and Minerals," and "Indian Baskets & Rings, Relics." The back of the parked trailer serves a display area for some of his wares including a long rifle supported by antlers, pistols, an animal skull, Indian baskets, rattlesnake skins and minerals.

Chuckawalla Slim (Edwin Howard  Vose II) sold minerals by the roadside in California and Arizona from 1925 to 1964. He ran his store along the roadside south of Cathedral City near Palm Springs in the winter.

title_tesim: Assembly at the Memorial Greek Ampitheatre, Santa Monica, 1937-1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SMHi Art Rm. Open House Nite. [Sleeve is an envelope addressed to Mr. Adelbert Bartlett with postal cancelation: "Los Angeles, Calif., Apr 25, 1937."]
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of an assembly at the Memorial Greek Ampitheatre on the Santa Monica High School campus. A speaker stands at a podium (in the pit below the stage) while two pairs of boys wearing uniforms stand on a walkway. The boy at left holds an American flag, the boy third from left holds what appears to be a German war ensign (Reichskriegsflagge). The flag is comprised of a white field with an offset black cross. A Prussian eagle encircled by a white disc rests at the center of the cross. A black, white, and red flag overlaid with a black iron cross sits in the upper left corner. Eight additional flags stand behind the boys. Student spectators sit in the amphitheater stands; band members are seated at foreground with their instruments and sheet music. Evidence of construction can be seen beyond the amphitheater where a scaffolding is viewed surrounding an adjacent building. Assembly photographed by Carolyn Bartlett.

Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: View towards the west entrance of the California Pacific Exposition from inside Balboa Park, with the tower of the Palace of Science (designed by the architect Bertram Goodhue for the Panama-California International Exposition of 1915) on the right. A woman is crossing the street. The road is lined with tidy rows of trees and the Veterans Foreign Wars Building is just visible through the trees on the right (constructed for the 1915 Exposition as the Science and Education Building, later known as the Palace of Photography and the Medical Arts Building. It was demolished in the 1960s).

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photographs of San Bernardino Society for California Pioneers, 1925, 1926, or 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2002 - 2003 - 2004 - 2005 2006 - 2007 - 2008 - 2009 San Bernardino Pioneers, Pioneers at their headquarters, 1926, 1928, Prints & Negs, Donkey missing

Text reads: San Bernardino Society of California Pioneers, 1925-1926.  The axe and the cross-cut of the old-time Westerner is again resounding at San Bernardino this week in the erecting by the Pioneer Society of San Bernardino County of a huge log cabin, an annex to the present smaller quarters of the pioneers, to house relics of covered wagon days.  C. J. Daley, president of the society, and John Brown Jr., secretary, may be seen notching logs for the corners of the great log structure which is arising in the city park a few [road?] [rows?] from the municipal auditorium.  Daley and Brown have as their able helpers John M. Case, past president, a skilful worker with adze and plane, and Will Mapstead, and John Bessant, clever manipulators of the long cross-cut saw.  Miles Benson, too, is right there in giving expert advice, not to mention lifting of the sawn logs into place on the rising cabin walls.  And of course laboring men must eat.  Women members of the pioneer society are busy preparing the noon meals at the fireplace in the present small cabin near by.  In the group are Mrs. Laura Case Clark, standing, Mrs. John M. Case, and Mrs. Floretta Sheets.  (Photo at fireplace)  On donkey are James Hancock and Joseph Murphy
named_subject_tesim: San Bernardino Society for California Pioneers
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photograph of Mrs. Laura Case Clark in log cabin, San Bernardino, 1926 or 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2002 - 2003 - 2004 - 2005 2006 - 2007 - 2008 - 2009 San Bernardino Pioneers, Pioneers at their headquarters, 1926, 1928, Prints & Negs, Donkey missing

Text reads: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Cal.  249R1964.  The Pioneer's Society of San Bernardino County, California, maintains a log club house in which primitive utensils of the covered wagon days are still used in preparing food for the regular monthly socials which include speech-making and old-time square dances and minuets as well as modern fox-trots not to mention the grand finale of supper with fried chicken, cake and trimmings.  In the picture is shown Mrs. Laura Case Clark, descendent of pioneers, tending a fire in the open grate in which an iron pot of water is being urged to boil for the coffee-making, while at her feet stands the iron "Dutch Oven" which contains bread baking from the heat of the blaze.  An old-fashioned churn stands beside Mrs. Clark.  At the right side of the fireplace are to be found a tallow candle dip, a bellows for blowing fires, an ax and a gold pan, utensils which were brought West in covered wagons 75 years ago.

caption_tesim: Text reads in part: SANTA MONICA(CALIF.) SCHOOL CHILDREN'S PLAQUE OFFICIALLY PRESENTED TO S.S.CONSTITUTION.  Santa Monica city officials yesterday visited San Diego where they dedicated a bronze plaque given recently by school children of that city to the S.S. Constitution.  The plaque commemorates the visit of 'Old Ironsides' to Santa Monica Bay in October of 1933.  At the ceremony the plaque which is the work of Tess Razelle, Santa Monica artist, was nailed to the mizzen mast of the frigate by Miss Alice Carter, daughter of the Santa Monica mayor, under the direction of commander Gulliver.  ...

title_tesim: Typewritten letter from Adelbert Bartlett requesting an invitation for Eugene R. Plummer to appear in Pioneer Days parade, 1936
caption_tesim: Text reads: IMPORTANT - FOR FRONT PAGE STORY WITH PICTURE 6/26/36 From Adelbert Bartlett.  Dear Mr. Warren: After two or three attempts to contact the gentleman whom I mentioned to you several days ago as one would should most certainly be seen this year in our Pioneer Days Parade, I have to report – Capt. Eugene R. Plummer, whose family at one time owned a total of some 4444.44 acres comprising the famous Rancho La Brea, upon which most of Hollywood now stands, would be glad to receive from our Pioneer Days Committee a written invitation to himself and to members of his “A La California Club” to attend our celebration. (It appears that this figure has been somewhat overlooked in former parades – not especially invited).Plummer, as you know, is known as the “father of Hollywood” and is one of the [“best-known” “x”-ed over] real pioneers in the film capital. The Hollywood Bowl [“stands” “x”-ed over with 8 “x”-es] occupies land once owned by Plummer. He has lived for 66 years on a piece of land which really is the last of the old Rancho La Brea to remain in his possession, [half a line “x”ed out] Historical groups have petitioned the State of California to make this site a state park. Plummer’s homestead is a perfect old-time hacienda, with grape-arbors, barbeque pits, spreading olive, eucalypti and elder-berry trees, at which descendents of [word “x”ed out] first Spanish-California families gather for fiestas and dances after the traditional manner. The man himself, in fact, is the embodiment of old-time California hospitality, and is always glad to receive visitors  and tell of [word “x”ed out] the Southland’s [word “x”ed out] glorious past. Capt, Plummer was present at the first auction of town lots in Santa Monica, A frequent visitor here through the years he has seen Santa Monica grow from grass-roots [word “x”ed out]  into a seaside metropolis.

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2361 - 2362 - 2 [sleeve torn, text missing] Eugene R. Plu [sleeve torn, text missing] Hollywood, Thomas Workman Temple. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. Col. Eugene R. Plummer, Hollywood's oldest settler, showing him with 1- On horseback with women members of family, descendents [sic] of Spanish pioneers 2- With guitar and Indian relics 3- Ranch windmill and patio of home. El Rey de la Vaqueros, Aug 1927

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Flower arrangement in shallow oval bowl. Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at  Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2595 P3
Text from negative sleeve: 2595 P 22 to 35 Preininger, Flowers, Los Angeles Junior College, Selected, 1935
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Small upright flowers on slender stems, arranged in 3 separate groups in shallow bowl on small table, with panels in background

Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

Keno Yamato, tiny artist's model from [struck out in pencil: the "Flowery Kingdom"] Japan who poses for members of the California Art Club which is located here.  One of the mechanistic-futuristic flower boxes of the patio of the Barnsdall castle (now home of the California Art Club) in background

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photograph of young women preparing for sunbathing, Santa Monica, circa 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Sun-Worshippers, Reshoot this

Text reads in part: Exclusive, For Outright Sale, 1391 to 1398.  SUN WORSHIPPERS PREPARE FOR BEACH SEASON.  After the manner of priestesses ... these modern sun-worshippers at the Deauville Beach, Los Angeles' exclusive ocean playground, anoint with oil and unguents ... Modern maids ... don the new low-back bathing suits ... to permit the sun rays to act upon every possible bit of body ... In the group are, left to right, Dorothy McQueen, Estelle Essex, Meredith Faulkner, Andray Hallehan, Texas Rose Doshier; kneeling, Dorothy Cornell.
